questions.prompt: Archive Clue: Which Library of Congress playlist contains fourteen 1901–1912 recordings spanning jubilee singing, spoken word, vaudeville, ragtime, art song, and opera?
questions.explanation: The National Jukebox Sampler contains fourteen selections recorded from 1901 through 1912 across those forms.

source.title: Playlist of Recordings | National Jukebox
source.evidenceSummary: The official playlist page describes National Jukebox Sampler as fourteen recordings from 1901 through 1912 covering jubilee singing, political and comic spoken word, vaudeville, ragtime, art song, and grand opera.

questions.prompt: Archive Clue: Which playlist gathers twelve early works linked to Will Marion Cook, Paul Laurence Dunbar, James Reese Europe, Noble Sissle, and Eubie Blake?
choices: Black Broadway and Tin Pan Alley
questions.explanation: Black Broadway and Tin Pan Alley highlights early African American songwriters and performers.

source.title: Playlist of Recordings | National Jukebox
source.evidenceSummary: The official page describes Black Broadway and Tin Pan Alley as twelve recordings of compositions by early African American composers and lyricists, naming Cook, Dunbar, Johnson, Europe, Sissle, and Blake.

questions.prompt: Archive Clue: Which playlist mixes ragtime, opera, Hawaiian music, a New York-dialect specialty, the Dinwiddie Colored Quartet, and Bert Williams?
questions.explanation: Gems from the Jukebox is the twelve-selection Victor playlist described by those clues.

source.title: Playlist of Recordings | National Jukebox
source.evidenceSummary: The official page describes Gems from the Jukebox as twelve Victor selections featuring ragtime, opera, Hawaiian music, a New York-dialect specialty, the Dinwiddie Colored Quartet, and Bert Williams.

questions.prompt: Inside a Shure SM58, which moving part generates the electrical signal as it travels through a magnetic field?
questions.explanation: The diaphragm moves the attached voice coil through the magnet’s field, generating the signal.

source.title: How They Did It: Inside the SM58
source.evidenceSummary: Shure explains that the SM58 uses a diaphragm, voice coil, and magnet; motion of the voice coil in the magnetic field generates the electrical signal.

questions.prompt: Which material did Shure say began replacing heat- and humidity-sensitive Rochelle salt in microphone transducers during the 1950s?
questions.explanation: Barium titanate ceramic retained its piezoelectric properties better under heat and humidity.

source.title: The History of Crystal Microphones and Artifacts from the Shure Archives
source.evidenceSummary: Shure states that ferroelectric ceramic barium titanate began replacing Rochelle salt in the 1950s because it did not lose piezoelectric characteristics in heat or humidity.

questions.prompt: In Shure’s account of the SM58’s broadcast origins, what does “SM” stand for?
questions.explanation: Shure says SM means Studio Microphone; the series was intended for broadcast studio use.

source.title: 10 Things You Might Not Know About the SM58
source.evidenceSummary: Shure explicitly says SM stands for Studio Microphone and explains that the SM series was developed for broadcast studio use in the early 1960s.

questions.prompt: Which entry is the outlier in the Library of Congress National Jukebox Popular Music subgenre list?
questions.explanation: Opera is under Classical Music; Blues, Whistling, and Yodeling are under Popular Music.

source.title: Genres | National Jukebox
source.evidenceSummary: The official genre guide places Opera beneath Classical Music and lists Blues, Whistling, and Yodeling beneath Popular Music.

questions.prompt: Which Shure model is the ceramic-microphone outlier rather than one of the crystal-microphone models named in the company history?
questions.explanation: Model 215 was Shure’s first ceramic microphone; the other named models used crystal transducers.

source.title: The History of Crystal Microphones and Artifacts from the Shure Archives
source.evidenceSummary: Shure identifies Models 70 and 71 as its first crystal microphones, describes Model 708A as a crystal microphone, and calls Model 215 its first ceramic microphone.

questions.prompt: Which item is the outlier among the three engineering elements Shure singles out as central to the SM58’s design?
questions.explanation: Shure names the diaphragm, transducer, and shock mount; a tape transport is not part of the microphone.

source.title: How They Did It: Inside the SM58
source.evidenceSummary: Shure says the SM58’s design can largely be attributed to three engineering elements: the diaphragm, transducer, and shock mount.
